description: If loving someone makes you happy, and a loves b, then a is happy.
goal: (-> (forall x (forall y (-> (App (App Loves x) y) (App Happy x)))) (-> (App (App Loves a) b) (App Happy a)))

guides.text: This problem bridges binary and unary [predicate|predicates]. $Loves(x,y)$ is a binary relation ('$x$ loves $y$'), written `(App (App Loves x) y)`. $Happy(x)$ is a unary property, written `(App Happy x)`. The premise says: for anyone $x$, if there's someone $y$ they love, then $x$ is happy. Given that $a$ loves $b$, prove $a$ is happy.
Assume the general principle: loving someone makes you happy. This is a formula with both a binary [predicate|predicate] ($Loves$) and a unary one ($Happy$).
guides.tactic: (assume (forall x (forall y (-> (App (App Loves x) y) (App Happy x)))))

guides.text: Now assume the specific fact: $a$ loves $b$. Written as nested `App`: `(App (App Loves a) b)`.
Instantiate the general principle with $a$ (replacing the outer $x$). This gives: for all $y$, if $a$ loves $y$, then $a$ is happy.
Instantiate with $b$ (replacing $y$). We get: if $a$ loves $b$, then $a$ is happy.
[modus-ponens|Modus ponens]: $a$ loves $b$ (step 2) plus the conditional (step 4) gives $Happy(a)$.
[discharge|Discharge] the inner assumption '$a$ loves $b$' to get $Loves(a,b) \to Happy(a)$.
[discharge|Discharge] the general principle to complete the proof. Notice how the binary predicate $Loves(x,y)$ and unary predicate $Happy(x)$ coexist naturally in the same proof.
guides.tactic: (->-intro (forall x (forall y (-> (App (App Loves x) y) (App Happy x)))) 6)
